summary: Gruber examines how many iPhones Apple could have rush-shipped to the US ahead of Trump's tariffs, building on Ryan Jones's back-of-napkin math. Using weight limits of freight planes and Apple's estimated 150,000 daily US iPhone sales, he calculates each plane carries roughly two days of inventory. Even five planes from India only amount to about 12 days of supply. He argues Apple's legendary just-in-time supply chain, which Tim Cook designed to minimize inventory, makes it structurally impossible to stockpile enough to meaningfully 'beat' tariffs. The piece concludes that Apple's enormous scale — 50 tons of iPhones sold daily in the US alone — makes any pre-tariff shipping a short-term hedge at best.
key_points: Each fully loaded freight plane can carry roughly two days' worth of US iPhone inventory, making pre-tariff stockpiling inherently limited
Apple sells approximately 150,000 iPhones per day in the US — about 50 tons — making the scale of the business staggering when viewed in physical terms
Apple's supply chain was deliberately designed by Tim Cook to minimize inventory, treating it as 'fundamentally evil' and managing it like dairy
Pre-tariff rush shipping could hedge against a few weeks of disruption at most, not a sustained tariff regime
Consumer demand may actually be elevated right now as buyers rush to purchase before anticipated price increases
money_quotes: 350,000 iPhones packed onto a single plane is a lot of iPhones. Sending a few million units across a dozen (or more!) planes is, quite literally, tons of iPhones. But Apple sells about 50 tons of new iPhones in the US alone every day.
There's no way Apple can 'beat' these Trump tariffs by having shipped products ahead of their taking effect.
We all know that Apple's iPhone business is huge. But when you start to consider it in practical terms like this it's just staggering.
You can't just rent random warehouse space to hold billions of dollars worth of iPhones.
Inventory, Cook has said, is 'fundamentally evil,' and he has been known to observe that it declines in value by 1% to 2% a week in normal times, faster in tough times like the present.

Apple's entire supply chain is rightly heralded as genius, a triumph of everything from component sourcing to assembly to transport. But the entire operation is premised on the continuous free flow of packaged products out of China.
key_insight: Apple's celebrated just-in-time supply chain, designed to hold minimal inventory, is precisely what makes it impossible to stockpile enough products to meaningfully outrun sustained tariffs.
